title: Whole-wheat sourdough
subtitle: Thirty percent wholemeal boule with overnight cold proof
excerpt: A free-form sourdough boule with 30% wholemeal flour for a slightly nutty flavour and open crumb. Naturally leavened with a ripe levain, bulk-fermented for 5 hours, then cold-proved overnight in a banneton. Baked covered in a Dutch oven at high heat.
sourceNotes: Wholemeal naturally leavened bread is documented across European bread traditions from antiquity. Eliza Acton, Modern Cookery for Private Families (1845), Project Gutenberg: the bread chapter discusses the addition of wholemeal flour to a starter-leavened dough for flavour and colour, noting that more than half wholemeal flour produces a heavier loaf. Mrs Beeton, Book of Household Management (1861), Project Gutenberg #10136: the chapter on household bread describes the blending of wholemeal and white flour in a naturally leavened dough to produce a loaf with more flavour than an all-white loaf but a lighter crumb than an all-wholemeal one.

content.text: Thirty percent wholemeal flour changes the character of a sourdough loaf without taking it over. The crumb stays open. The flavour becomes a little nutty. The crust has more colour than an all-white loaf. At higher wholemeal levels the gluten network weakens and the crumb closes. 30% is the sweet spot for a home baker who wants a clearly wholegrain loaf without losing structure.

Build your levain 4 hours before mixing: combine 50 g of active {{sourdough-starter}} with 50 g of the {{strong-bread-flour}} and 50 g of water. It is ready when roughly doubled and bubbly throughout.

Combine both flours and 340 ml of the {{water}} in a large bowl. Mix until no dry flour remains. Cover and leave for 30 minutes, the wholemeal flour absorbs water more slowly than white flour, and this rest gives it time to hydrate before the starter goes in.

Pour the ripe {{sourdough-starter}} over the dough. Dissolve the {{sea-salt-fine}} in the remaining 20 ml of water and add. Work everything together with wet hands until the starter is fully incorporated.

Cover and ferment at room temperature for 5 hours. During the first 2 hours, perform 4 sets of stretch and fold, one set every 30 minutes. After the 4th set, leave the dough undisturbed. At the end of bulk, the dough should have grown by 60 to 70%, feel light when you lift it, and show visible bubbles at the surface.

Dust the banneton generously with rice flour or wholemeal flour. Tip the dough onto an unfloured surface. Using a bench scraper, fold the dough in from four sides to build tension, then flip it over and drag it toward you in a circular motion to tighten the surface. Lower it seam-side up into the banneton. Cover with a shower cap or clingfilm and refrigerate for 8 to 12 hours. This is the
: the slow, cold fermentation develops flavour and makes the dough easier to score.

Preheat the oven to 240°C fan with the Dutch oven inside for at least 45 minutes. Take the dough directly from the fridge. Turn it out onto a piece of baking paper, score the surface with a deep cross or single arc cut. Using the baking paper as a sling, lower the dough into the hot Dutch oven, put the lid on, and bake for 20 minutes covered. Remove the lid, reduce to 220°C, and bake for a further 20 to 25 minutes until the crust is very dark and the internal temperature reads 97°C. Cool on a rack for at least 1 hour before cutting.
